title: Managing a flock through the moult
subtitle: What is happening, why egg production stops, and how to support the birds.
excerpt: Autumn moult is not a health problem. It is a normal annual cycle in which hens replace their feathers before winter and, as a consequence, stop laying for four to twelve weeks. Understanding the moult helps you manage feed, avoid unnecessary intervention, and know when a hen that is not moulting is actually failing to lay for a different reason.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Practice for the Welfare of Laying Hens, 2018. Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive. University of Wisconsin Extension, Backyard Poultry Series.

glossaryTerms.definition: The annual process by which birds shed and replace their feathers, driven by changes in day length. In laying hens, moult causes a temporary cessation of egg production; the protein requirements during feather re-growth are high.

New feathers emerging from the follicle, encased in a blood-filled sheath. They are sensitive to the touch and appear as short dark quills before unfurling. Hens in heavy moult show visible pin feathers across the back, neck, and wings.

A rapid, heavy feather loss in which a hen loses much of her plumage within a few weeks and regrows it over the same period. Hens in a hard moult stop laying entirely. A soft moult is gradual and the hen may continue laying at reduced rate.

content.text: Each autumn, as daylight drops below 12 hours, laying hens begin to

: they shed their feathers and grow a fresh set. This requires a large proportion of the protein and energy that would otherwise go into egg production, so laying stops. The moult is not a management failure; it is how the bird prepares for winter.
What a moulting hen looks like
The first sign is a sharp drop in egg numbers in late September or October. Then feathers appear in the bedding and run, often in larger quantities than you expect. A hen in
will look alarming: bare patches on the neck, back, and breast, with
emerging across the body. This is normal. She will grow her feathers back over four to eight weeks.

The main requirement is extra protein. Switch the flock to a grower or maintenance ration (typically 18-20% protein) from their layer pellets for the duration of the moult. Some keepers offer supplementary protein in the form of dried mealworms or soaked cat food; both work but should be fed as a supplement, not a replacement for their formulated ration.

Do not handle moulting hens unnecessarily. Pin feathers are blood-filled and sensitive; rough handling causes pain and can break the quill, causing bleeding. Routine checks should be visual rather than physical during a heavy moult.
Separating moult from other non-laying causes

Not every hen that stops laying in autumn is moulting. Check each bird: a hen in moult will have visible feather loss. A hen that is not moulting but is also not laying may be carrying a soft-shelled egg, hiding a nest, coming into a broody cycle, or approaching the end of productive life. These require different responses.

Most hens return to laying in November or December, once their new feathers are fully grown. The first eggs back may be slightly smaller or pale-shelled while the reproductive system restarts. Hens that have completed their second or third moult typically lay at a lower rate than their first-season peak, but the eggs are often larger.

If a hen has not resumed laying by the end of January, assess her overall condition: weight, comb colour, vent moisture, and behaviour. A hen in good condition that is simply not laying in winter may be responding normally to low day length; supplement with artificial light (14-16 hours per day) if you need year-round production, or accept the winter gap as the natural pattern.
